region_of_origin: Middle East / Europe (historically associated with Lübeck, Germany and Toledo, Spain)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for marzipan as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for confectionery is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of marzipan is approximately 30 g (one small piece or confection), as consumed as a sweet treat or baking decoration.

nutrient_source.title: Marzipan — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Marzipan (F07010004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169120, Marzipan) to supply saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g fields not present in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for marzipan was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using the closest analogous food: almond-based confection with high sugar content. Marzipan is approximately 47.8g carbohydrate/100g with 36.3g sugar; the GI is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the sugar-dominant carbohydrate profile moderated by almond fibre (4.8g/100g) and protein (9g/100g). Glycemic load per 30g serving = (65 × 47.81 × 0.30) / 100 = 9.3g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (36.25g/100g) with low sodium (9mg/100g) and beneficial potassium (314mg/100g); sugar excess is the primary concern
hypertension.guidance: Marzipan has very low sodium (9mg/100g) and useful potassium (314mg/100g) which are favourable for blood pressure, but the very high sugar content (36.25g/100g) promotes insulin resistance and weight gain that worsen hypertension; limit to occasional small portions of 10–15g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 36.25g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; estimated GI of 65 (medium) with GL of 9.3 per 30g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marzipan is unsuitable for regular consumption in Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (36.25g/100g); if consumed at all, restrict to a single small piece (10–15g) as an occasional treat and monitor postprandial glucose response, noting that almond-derived fibre (4.8g/100g) and protein (9g/100g) provide only partial glycaemic moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (27.74g/100g) predominantly from almonds (MUFA-rich); low saturated fat (2.1g/100g) and zero cholesterol are favourable, but high sugar load is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of marzipan is predominantly monounsaturated (almond-derived, ~17.8g/100g) with low saturated fat (2.1g/100g) and zero cholesterol, which are neutral-to-favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sugar content (36.25g/100g) raises triglycerides and promotes inflammation; consume only occasionally in small portions (10–15g).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (36.25g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, overriding the anti-inflammatory benefit of almond-derived MUFA and vitamin E
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite the almond base providing anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats and vitamin E, the dominant sugar content (36.25g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation and is unsuitable for regular consumption in rheumatoid arthritis; plain almonds are a far preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marzipan is a processed confection made primarily from finely ground almonds and sugar, typically combined with glucose syrup and sometimes rose water or egg white to form a smooth, pliable paste. It has a dense, soft texture with a characteristic sweet almond flavour and a pale beige to ivory colour. Marzipan is used both as a standalone sweet and as a baking ingredient — it is commonly moulded into decorative shapes, used as a cake covering or filling, or incorporated into chocolates and pastries. Historically associated with European confectionery traditions, particularly in Germany (Lübeck marzipan) and Spain (Toledo mazapán), it is now consumed internationally. Nutritionally, marzipan per 100g provides 458 kcal, 9g protein, 27.74g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from almonds), 47.81g carbohydrate, and 36.25g sugar. The almond base contributes 4.8g dietary fibre, 172mg calcium, 314mg potassium, and 1.6mg iron. Sodium is very low at 9mg/100g. Clinically, the very high sugar content (36.25g/100g) makes marzipan unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing hypertension through dietary sugar restriction. The MUFA-rich fat profile and low saturated fat are relatively favourable for cardiovascular health, but the sugar load remains a concern for triglyceride levels. Marzipan is a tree-nut product and is a significant allergen for individuals with almond or tree-nut allergies. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of marzipan?
Can I eat marzipan if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between marzipan and almond paste?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F07010004) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (marzipan, FDC ID 169120). GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI sub-field only) as no direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for marzipan. Overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match for all primary nutrients.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (17.8g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (5.9g/100g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169120, Marzipan) as the HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. Trans fat is 0.0g confirmed per USDA source. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for marzipan are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F07010004, and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169120) does not provide complete micronutrient coverage for this item as of 2026; data unavailability confirmed by consulting both approved sources: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 (data not available) per the Veritas numeric hygiene rule; they are not zero and are not estimated. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the sugar-dominant carbohydrate profile of marzipan moderated by almond fibre and protein; no direct GI measurement for marzipan was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. This should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0mg — confirmed zero per HPB source; marzipan contains no animal-derived fat in its standard formulation. 5. FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301710) and FoodEx2 code (A0EJM) are cited as defensible matches; SNOMED CT has no identified code for marzipan as a food item.
